summary:
The Macedonian Dynasty (867–1056 AD) represented the golden age of the Byzantine Empire, a nearly two-century period of military expansion, administrative consolidation, intellectual flowering, and missionary activity that made Byzantium the most powerful Christian state in the world and established Orthodox Christianity's enduring presence across Eastern Europe and Russia. The dynasty was founded by Basil I, a Macedonian peasant who rose through palace intrigue to murder his way to the throne — an inauspicious beginning for a dynasty that would produce several of Byzantium's greatest rulers.

Under the Macedonian emperors, the Byzantine Empire recovered territories in Italy, the Balkans, Syria, and Mesopotamia that had been lost to Arab, Bulgar, and Viking pressure. The emperors Nikephoros II Phokas, John I Tzimiskes, and [[event:3517|Basil II]] conducted aggressive offensive campaigns, capturing Antioch (969 AD), extending Byzantine suzerainty into the Caucasus, and ultimately destroying the First Bulgarian Empire. The Macedonian period also saw remarkable intellectual achievement: the emperor Constantine VII Porphyrogennetos authored major works of history and court ceremonial, and the court sponsored systematic compilations of ancient Greek learning — encyclopedias of history, military science, agriculture, and veterinary medicine — that preserved classical knowledge. The missionary work of Constantine (Cyril) and Methodius in Great Moravia, the conversion of the Bulgarians (864 AD), and the Orthodox baptism of [[event:2149|Kievan Rus]] (988 AD) under Basil II's patronage permanently shaped the religious and cultural map of Eastern Europe, creating the Orthodox Christian civilization that would define Russia, Serbia, Bulgaria, and Romania.

figures.name: Alexander the Great
figures.summary: King of Macedon from 336 to 323 BC
